# corpus-tool fixture: notes on caching build artifacts

This document is a golden fixture for the `corpus-tool` test suite,
written to stand in for the register of typical small-model blog output:
a little more hedged, a little more evenly paced, than the human fixture
next to it. It is not the output of any real model; it was written by
hand to exercise the `llm` manifest path (model, prompt id, and
generation config all populated).

Caching build artifacts sounds simple until a project actually tries it.
The first attempt usually caches everything, keyed on a hash of the
whole source tree. This works, technically, but it invalidates on almost
every change, so the cache hit rate stays low and the complexity is not
paying for itself.

A better approach keys the cache on the actual inputs of each step,
rather than the whole tree. If a compilation step only reads three
files, the cache key should depend on those three files, not on every
file in the repository. This is more work to set up, but it pays off
quickly: unrelated changes elsewhere in the tree stop invalidating
unrelated caches, and the hit rate climbs.

It also helps to separate the cache key from the cache content. The key
can be a short hash; the content can be stored under that hash in a flat
directory, so lookups stay fast even as the cache grows. Garbage
collection then becomes a simple sweep: keep whatever was touched in the
last N builds, discard the rest.

One more detail worth calling out: cache correctness matters more than
cache speed. A fast cache that occasionally returns stale output is
worse than no cache at all, because the failure is silent and hard to
reproduce. Any caching layer should be built so that a cache miss is
always safe, and a cache hit is only ever taken when the key fully
captures everything the step depends on. That constraint is worth
protecting even when it costs a little performance, because debugging a
stale-cache bug after the fact costs far more than the performance would
have.
